## Reversibility of Redox Reactions in Electrochemical Cells


1. In chemistry, a **redox **reaction involves the **transfer **of electrons between two species. 
2. One species is **oxidized **(loses electrons), while the other is **reduced **(gains electrons). 
3. In some cases, these reactions are **reversible**, meaning the products can be **converted back **into reactants by applying an external energy source, such as an electric current.

### How Reversibility Works


1. **Reversibility **in redox reactions is the basis of secondary (rechargeable) electrochemical cells. 
2. During **discharge**, the cell converts **chemical **energy into **electrical **energy through spontaneous redox reactions. 
3. When an** external voltage** is applied during charging, the redox reactions are **reversed**, **restoring the reactants**.
  1. **Discharge: Spontaneous **redox reactions produce electrical energy.
  2. **Charge: Non-spontaneous** redox reactions occur, driven by external electrical energy.

<callout type="tip">To reverse a redox reaction in a rechargeable battery, the **applied voltage must be slightly greater than the cell’s standard voltage** to overcome energy losses due to resistance and inefficiencies.</callout>

<callout type="self_review">What is the difference between a spontaneous and a non-spontaneous redox reaction?</callout>

## Examples of Reversible Redox Systems


### **Lead-Acid Batteries**


1. **Lead-acid batteries** are commonly used in cars and backup power systems. 
2. These batteries consist of a **lead **anode (negative electrode) and a **lead(IV) oxide** cathode (positive electrode) immersed in sulfuric acid.

#### **Discharge (Powering the Device)**


During discharge, the following reactions occur:

1. **Anode (Oxidation): **$$ \text{Pb(s)} + \text{HSO}_4^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4(s) + \text{H}^+(aq) + 2e^- $$
2. **Cathode (Reduction): **$$ \text{PbO}_2(s) + 3\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{HSO}_4^-(aq) + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) $$
3. **Overall Cell Reaction: **$$ \text{Pb(s)} + \text{PbO}_2(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) $$

![Diagram of a lead acid cell showing Pb anode and PbO2 cathode in sulfuric acid with electrons flowing through a load.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1c0c0d9e5b3924941e6e8ccd622aff4d166ad16e-2859x2316.png)

#### **Charge (Recharging the Battery)**


When an external voltage is applied, the reactions are reversed:

1. **Anode (Reduction): **$$ \text{PbSO}_4(s) + \text{H}^+(aq) + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Pb(s)} + \text{HSO}_4^-(aq) $$
2. **Cathode (Oxidation): **$$ \text{PbSO}_4(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{PbO}_2(s) + 3\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{HSO}_4^-(aq) + 2e^- $$
3. **Overall Cell Reaction: **$$ 2\text{PbSO}_4(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{Pb(s)} + \text{PbO}_2(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4(aq) $$

<callout type="example">For instance, when you start your car, the lead-acid battery discharges to power the starter motor. As the engine runs, the alternator recharges the battery by reversing the redox reactions.</callout>

### **Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd) Cells**


1. **Nickel-cadmium cells** are another type of rechargeable battery, often used in portable electronic devices and power tools. 
2. These batteries consist of a cadmium anode and a nickel(III) oxyhydroxide (NiO(OH)) cathode in an alkaline electrolyte, usually potassium hydroxide.

#### **Discharge (Powering the Device)**


During discharge, the reactions are:

1. **Anode (Oxidation): **$$ \text{Cd(s)} + 2\text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2(s) + 2e^- $$
2. **Cathode (Reduction): **$$ \text{NiO(OH)}(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2(s) + \text{OH}^-(aq) $$
3. **Overall Cell Reaction: **$$ \text{Cd(s)} + 2\text{NiO(OH)}(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2(s) + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2(s) $$

#### **Charge (Recharging the Battery)**


When an external voltage is applied, the reactions are reversed:

1. **Anode (Reduction): **$$ \text{Cd(OH)}_2(s) + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cd(s)} + 2\text{OH}^-(aq) $$
2. **Cathode (Oxidation): **$$ \text{Ni(OH)}_2(s) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{NiO(OH)}(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + e^- $$
3. **Overall Cell Reaction: **$$ \text{Cd(OH)}_2(s) + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2(s) \rightarrow \text{Cd(s)} + 2\text{NiO(OH)}(s) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) $$

<callout type="note">* NiCd batteries are durable and can endure many charge-discharge cycles, but they suffer from the "memory effect," where incomplete discharges reduce their capacity over time. 
* To reduce apparent capacity loss, avoid repeated shallow cycling to the same level and follow the manufacturer’s recommended charge and maintenance procedure.</callout>

### **Lithium-Ion Batteries**


1. **Lithium-ion batteries** are commonly used in portable electronics, electric vehicles, and energy storage systems due to their high energy density and rechargeability. 
2. These batteries consist of a **lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO₂)** cathode and a **graphite** anode, with a lithium salt electrolyte.

#### **Discharge (Powering the Device)**


During discharge, lithium ions move from the anode to the cathode, generating electrical energy:

1. **Anode (Oxidation):**  
$$\text{LiC}_6 \rightarrow C_6 + \text{Li}^+ + e^-$$
2. **Cathode (Reduction): $$\text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + x\text{Li}^+ + x e^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2$$**
3. **Overall Cell Reaction: $$\text{LiC}_6 + \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 \rightarrow C_6 + \text{LiCoO}_2$$**

#### **Charge (Recharging the Battery)**


During charging, an external power source drives the reverse reactions, storing energy by moving lithium ions back to the anode:

1. **Anode (Reduction):**  
$$C_6 + \text{Li}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{LiC}_6$$
2. **Cathode (Oxidation): $$\text{LiCoO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + x\text{Li}^+ + x e^-$$**
3. **Overall Cell Reaction: $$C_6 + \text{LiCoO}_2 \rightarrow \text{LiC}_6 + \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2$$**

![Schematic of a lithium ion cell with a graphite anode, LiCoO2 cathode, and polymer electrolyte showing electron and Li+ flow through a load.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/81b9c3d6701c6e7641395ffb32e07010299b9fcf-2859x2316.png)

### **Advantages and Disadvantages of Fuel Cells, Primary Cells, and Secondary Cells**


|  | Fuel cells | Primary cells | Secondary cells |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Advantages | High efficiency, low emissions (water as the only byproduct in hydrogen fuel cells), and continuous operation as long as fuel is supplied. | Convenient, portable, and long shelf life, making them ideal for single-use devices like remote controls and flashlights. | Reusable through multiple charge cycles, reducing waste and long-term costs, commonly used in laptops, phones, and electric vehicles. |
| Disadvantages | High production costs, storage challenges for hydrogen gas, and reliance on rare catalysts like platinum. | Non-rechargeable, contributing to electronic waste, and limited energy capacity. | Higher initial cost, capacity degradation over time, and some materials (e.g., lithium) can be environmentally harmful. |

<callout type="self_review">* How do rechargeable batteries contribute to reducing fossil fuel dependency in transportation and energy storage?
* Why do rechargeable batteries eventually fail, even though their reactions are theoretically reversible? Can you propose solutions to extend their lifespan?</callout>
